CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (background on HRG/MSOL, definition of RGG, parsing algorithm) and states its goals (investigate RGLs, closure under intersection, and an efficient parser) early on. However the provided text contains many duplicated paragraphs, citation and LaTeX artifacts, and occasional sentence-fragmenting that make sections harder to follow and suggest the manuscript needs careful copy-editing. Notation and formal definitions (e.g. of HRG, paths, RGG constraints) are mostly present and clear, and figures/tables are relevant, but poor formatting and repetition reduce their effectiveness and the overall readability.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem for NLP and structured prediction — defining probabilistic, intersection-closed graph language families suitable for semantic graphs (motivated by AMR and similar resources) as discussed in the introduction (§1) and dataset motivation. By showing that regular graph languages (RGLs) are a subfamily of both HRL and MSOL and proving closure under intersection while providing an efficient (linear-time) top-down parsing algorithm (§2 and §4; “We prove that RGLs are closed under intersection and provide an efficient parsing algorithm, with runtime linear in the size of the input graph”), the work gives a practically useful formalism that could change how researchers model probabilistic distributions over graphs. It also opens substantive research directions (probabilistic RGLs, grammar induction, relaxed constraints) while acknowledging expressivity limitations of RGGs for some semantic derivations (e.g., the grammar in Table 1 may be “unnatural” and “RGG is so constrained that it may not be expressive enough”), which somewhat limits immediate broad adoption but still yields a strong, reusable contribution.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies and extends Courcelle’s regular graph grammars (RGL; Courcelle 1991) by developing a probabilistic formulation and proving closure under intersection while giving an efficient top-down Earley-like parsing algorithm with runtime linear in the input graph (claims summarized in the text: “This paper investigates the regular graph languages... We prove that RGLs are closed under intersection and provide an efficient parsing algorithm, with runtime linear...”). These contributions constitute a notable new combination and algorithmic advance for RGLs in an NLP context, but build on established ingredients (probabilistic HRGs: Booth & Thompson 1973; HRG parsing generalizations: Chiang et al. 2013; predictive top-down ideas: Drewes et al. 2015), so the work is clearly novel but not a wholly new theory.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper directly addresses probabilistic grammars and parsing for graph-structured semantic representations (e.g. AMR) which are of clear interest to the NLP community (see discussion of AMR and machine translation in the introduction and Table 1/Fig.4). It develops a grammar family (RGL/RGG), proves closure properties (intersection) and gives a parsing algorithm (generalizing Earley, with runtime claims) — methods and formal standards appropriate for top-tier NLP venues. The work is positioned against relevant prior work (HRG, MSOL, DAG automata) and explicitly motivates NLP applications (semantic parsing and generation), so the intended audience and problem formulation match the venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper appears largely theoretical and provides formal definitions, a parsing algorithm and complexity claims (e.g. linear runtime) with references to proofs and supplementary material, so there is moderate reproducibility for a theory contribution but some implementation details are deferred to prior work/supplementary material. There are no experiments, empirical evaluations, or ablation/sensitivity analyses presented to support practical claims about parsing performance or applicability. Related work is discussed extensively (HRL, MSOL, DAGAL, Courcelle, etc.), and limitations are acknowledged (e.g. RGG’s expressivity limits and the ‘unnatural’ grammar in Table 1). Overall the analysis is sound for a theoretical paper but lacks empirical validation and implementation-level detail.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The manuscript asserts key technical claims — that RGLs are closed under intersection and that there is a linear-time top-down parsing algorithm — but the provided text contains no complete proofs or rigorous complexity analysis supporting these claims (the paper only cites Courcelle for RGL↦MSOL and sketches a parser using boundary representations). The statement “Since RGLs are a subfamily of both HRL and MSOL, they inherit probability and intersection closure, respectively” is logically weak (being a subset of MSOL does not by itself guarantee closure of the subset under intersection). No experiments or empirical validation are given for the probabilistic aspects. These omissions are significant technical gaps that undermine the main claims as presented here.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper situates itself well within prior work, citing and discussing the main competing formalisms (HRL, DAG automata/MSOL, Courcelle’s RGL) and relevant papers (e.g. Booth & Thompson 1973; Courcelle 1991; Chiang et al. 2013; Drewes et al. 2015; Quernheim & Knight 2012), and it clearly contrasts their properties (expressivity, closure under intersection, probabilistic modeling) and parsing approaches (e.g. CKY vs Earley generalizations). It also acknowledges limitations of RGG (e.g. possibly too constrained) and relates to other restricted HRG variants (TLG, RDG). However, the comparisons are largely theoretical: no empirical baselines or experiments are presented to compare practical performance, so empirical fairness/metrics are not addressed.
